top.title: Joule-Heated direct writing: an electrically-driven additive manufacturing paradigm for space fabrication
top.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-46686-5 Joule-Heated direct writing: an electrically-driven additive manufacturing paradigm for space fabrication
top.content:
Abstract

The imperative for in-space manufacturing drives the development of compact, energy-efficient metal additive manufacturing (AM) technologies. This work introduces and investigates a novel Joule-Heating Additive Manufacturing (JHAM) process, inspired by the Joule–Lenz effect, for the direct writing of metallic structures. A fully coupled thermal-electrical-structural finite element model is developed to simulate the multilayer deposition of 304 stainless steel wire, elucidating the unique "tusk-shaped" temperature field evolution and the effects of current, speed, and contact width. Single-bead, five-layer walls are fabricated experimentally, and the influence of process parameters on macrostructure, geometry (width, height, overlap rate), surface roughness (Ra, Rz), and cross-sectional hardness is systematically characterized. The results demonstrate that optimal macroscopic quality is achieved at 120 A, 200 mm/min, and a 1.0 mm contact width. The forming width and overlap rate increase with current, while the forming height decreases; conversely, increasing speed reduces width and overlap but increases height. Surface roughness and hardness exhibit non-monotonic trends with varying parameters, with a minimum Ra of 0.142 µm attained. Simulated and experimentally measured voltages show good agreement, validating the multiphysics model.Validation results indicate a voltage discrepancy of less than 10% between simulation and experiment. The JHAM process, with its low power consumption (<1 kW) and simplified architecture, presents a promising candidate for on-orbit fabrication of metallic components.

Similar content being viewed by others

Introduction

The advancement of long duration space exploration and the establishment of extraterrestrial habitats necessitate a paradigm shift from ground-based manufacturing to on-orbit, on-demand fabrication1,2,3,4. This transition is crucial in order to overcome the severe constraints of payload mass, volume, and associated launch costs. Additive manufacturing (AM) is widely recognized as a key enabling technology for this shift, as it offers the potential for in-situ repair, recycling, and production of strategic components5,6,7,8,9.

Global space agencies, including NASA, ESA, and the China Manned Space Agency, have active research programs focused on adapting AM for the space environment10,11. The overarching goal is to evolve the supporting infrastructure from “transporting everything” to "manufacturing what is needed" on-site12,13. Various studies have explored specific space AM applications, ranging from electron beam freeform fabrication and micro-droplet jetting to variable-gravity forming of polymers and space trusses14,15,16,17,18,19,20.

Current mainstream metal AM heat sources for terrestrial use—such as lasers21,22,23,24,25,26, arcs27,28,29,30,31,32, electron beams33,34,35,36,37,38, and plasma arcs39,40—present challenges for space applications, including high power demand, complex optics, and vacuum requirements. To address these limitations, we propose a Joule-Heating Additive Manufacturing (JHAM) technique. Based on the fundamental Joule-Lenz law, JHAM utilizes the electrical resistance of the metal wire itself as the heat source for melting, offering inherent advantages of simplicity, low energy consumption, compactness, and gravity-independent operation.

Previous research on resistance-based AM has explored mechanisms of wire melting, droplet transfer, and single-layer formation. However, a comprehensive understanding of the multi-physics phenomena governing multi-layer deposition-encompassing dynamic heat generation, complex thermal history, and their resultant effects on geometry, surface integrity, and mechanical properties remains limited. Accurate numerical simulation of this process is challenging due to the moving contact zone and strong coupling between electrical, thermal, and mechanical fields.

Although our previous research41 demonstrated the feasibility of using Joule heating for single-layer droplet transfer, it did not address the cumulative thermal effects and structural stability required for 3D manufacturing. This study has made significant progress, focusing on the multi-layer deposition process and adopting a continuous roller structure as the in-situ hot rolling tool, as shown in Fig. 1

Experimental setup

Single-bead, five-layer walls were fabricated using 304 stainless steel wire (0.3 mm diameter). The upper electrode utilized a copper alloy roller (outer diameter: 12mm; inner diameter: 6mm; thickness: 8mm ). A constant mechanical contact force of 0.3 N was maintained throughout the deposition process using a spring-loaded tensioning system, ensuring both stable electrical conductivity and the aforementioned in-situ burnishing effect. While Table 4 lists the discrete parameter nodes utilized for the initial finite element simulation to establish the process window, the experimental validation explored a broader, continuous range of parameters to systematically capture the morphological evolution.The experimental setup consisted of a vacuum chamber, a wire feeding system, and a power supply, as shown in Fig. 2a. Surface roughness was measured using confocal laser scanning microscope (Keyence VK-X200, Japan) at the locations indicated in Fig. 2b.

Numerical simulation

A transient three-dimensional finite element model was developed, as shown in Fig. 3a, for simulating the layer-by-layer deposition process of five layers of metal wires. The meshing is shown in Fig. 3b. This process was decomposed into seven analysis steps: applying pressure, maintaining, applying current, roller movement (deposition), current removal, cooling, and pressure release (Fig. 3c). The model includes temperature-related material properties (Tables 1-3) and contact resistance. The material properties used in the simulation are listed in Tables 1, 2, and 3. The process parameters are detailed in Table 4.

To accurately capture the melting and solidification of the 304 stainless steel wire, the phase change process and the associated latent heat were incorporated using the equivalent heat capacity method. The latent heat of fusion (\(\text{L}=260 kJ/kg\)) is distributed over a solidus-liquidus temperature range (from \({\text{T}}_{\text{s}}=1400{\hspace{0.25em}}^{\circ }C\) to \({\text{T}}_{\text{l}}=1450{\hspace{0.25em}}^{\circ }C\)). This ensures that the energy balance during the solid-to-liquid transition is correctly conserved in the thermal field calculations.

The contact resistance at the roller-wire and wire-substrate interfaces plays a critical role in Joule heat generation. As shown in Table 3, the electrical contact resistance is modeled as a temperature-dependent variable. It progressively decreases as the local temperature increases, which physically reflects the material softening, the increase in actual contact area under constant pressure, and the eventual melting at the interface.

Given the simulated in-space vacuum environment, convective heat transfer is entirely neglected. Thermal dissipation is governed solely by thermal conduction within the solid structures and thermal radiation to the ambient environment. The radiation boundary condition is applied to all free surfaces governed by the Stefan-Boltzmann law, with an assumed ambient temperature of \(20{\hspace{0.25em}}^{\circ }C\). The surface emissivity (\(\upvarepsilon\)) is set to \(0.4\) for the stainless steel and \(0.6\) for the copper roller. Mechanically, the bottom surface of the substrate is fully fixed, while a dynamic mechanical pressure is applied by the top roller.

To ensure numerical accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency, a mesh convergence test was conducted prior to the primary simulations. Three different mesh densities were evaluated for the deposition zone. The results indicated that refining the minimum element size below \(0.1 mm\) yielded a peak temperature variation of less than \(5\text{\%}\). Consequently, this optimal mesh density was adopted for the deposition region to capture the localized steep temperature gradients accurately.

Results and discussion

Temperature field evolution

The simulated temperature field evolution reveals a characteristic "tusk-shaped" profile (Fig. 2). During the roller movement step, the highest temperature region is not static; it initiates at the contact interface between the newest (fifth) and the previous (fourth) layer, then shifts rearward relative to the roller’s motion. The formation of this asymmetric profile is governed by competing heat transfer mechanisms: the copper roller acts as a significant heat sink, inducing rapid quenching at the contact front.Under vacuum conditions, the absence of convective heat transfer fundamentally alters the thermal dissipation characteristics. At the contact front, the copper roller serves as a primary heat sink, inducing rapid quenching. Conversely, heat dissipation at the rear is governed exclusively by thermal radiation and conduction through the built wall. This imbalance leads to a localized heat accumulation behind the moving roller, which is the primary driver for the asymmetric tusk-shaped temperature field.This directional heat flow helps stabilize the deposition by preventing the re-melting of previously solidified layers. The isothermal surfaces are dense beneath the roller due to rapid conduction and sparse behind it due to residual heat, creating the asymmetric, elongated profile. See Fig. 4

The ivory-like temperature field results from the increasing number of wire layers. When heat transfer and radiation are ignored, heat generation occurs only in the contact region beneath the roller, which moves with the roller (Fig. 5a).When heat transfer and radiation are considered but roller–substrate heat transfer is ignored, the temperature field trails behind the roller movement (Fig. 5b).When roller–substrate heat transfer is also considered (Fig. 5c), the larger roller volume causes region A to lose most of its heat to the roller, making it cooler than the heat-producing zone. Region C is warmer than region A due to combined prior and current heat production. Region B, despite similar combined heating, loses more heat to the roller than downward, making it cooler than region C. Region D, being closer to the heat source, is slightly warmer than region C. Thus, the temperature order is A < B < C < D, forming the ivory-like field in Fig. 5d.

A parametric study quantified the effects of current (I), speed (V), and contact width (W) on the peak temperature and melt zone extent (Fig. 6, Table 4). Within the studied range, every 10 A increase in current raised the peak temperature by approximately 300°C. Increasing the speed by 100 mm/min reduced the peak temperature by about 150°C, as the heating time per unit length decreased. Increasing the contact width by 0.2 mm reduced the peak temperature by roughly 200°C, due to a decrease in current density for a fixed current. Critically, the simulations identify parameter windows that achieve sufficient melting for bonding without excessive heat input that causes collapse or excessive remelting of lower layers.

Macrostructure and geometry analysis

Figure 7 presents the macroscopic top-view morphologies of the single-bead five-layer walls fabricated under varying process parameters (current: 100–140 A, speed: 100–300 mm/min, and section width: 0.6–1.4 mm). The results demonstrate that the optimal macroscopic quality, characterized by a flat surface devoid of deformation or gaps, is achieved at a current of 120 A, a speed of 200 mm/min, and a section width of 1.0 mm. Deviations from these optimal values led to significant degradation in forming quality. Specifically, fluctuations in current or section width altered the degree of wire melting, shifting the morphology from under-melted (poor adhesion) to over-melted (collapse). Similarly, variations in deposition speed disrupted the stability of the layer formation, resulting in inconsistent surface quality.

The impact of varying current, speed, and section width on the cross-sectional morphology, forming size, and overlap rate of single-bead multilayer walls is depicted in Fig. 8a, b, and c. Within a certain range, a gradual increase in current enhances the forming width of a single-bead multilayer wall while simultaneously reducing the forming height. However, the change in width is significantly less pronounced than that in height, and the overlap rate progressively increases due to the heightened heat production associated with larger currents.Physically, the increase in current leads to higher Joule heat generation (\(Q={I}^{2}Rt\)), which raises the melt pool temperature. This elevated temperature reduces the viscosity and surface tension of the molten metal, thereby enhancing its fluidity and wettability. Consequently, the molten metal spreads more readily across the substrate, resulting in a wider bead and a reduced layer height. On the other hand, increasing the speed significantly reduces the bonding rate, leading to a decrease in the forming width and an increase in the forming height. This shift is attributed to the reduction in linear heat input (Joules per mm) as speed increases. With less thermal energy delivered per unit length, the volume of the molten pool decreases, and the solidification rate accelerates. The molten metal solidifies before it can fully spread outwards, leading to a narrower width and a piled-up morphology. Additionally, increasing the section width solely increases the forming height, with both the forming width and overlap rate decreasing. Notably, a shift from a 0.9 mm to a 1.0 mm section width resulted in the most significant decrease in the overlap rate, decreasing by 38.71% compared to the preceding period. This decrease is due to the increased section width and decreased current density, leading to dispersed heat production and reduced wire melting.

Surface roughness and hardness analysis

The surface quality of the contact surface is crucial in JHAM, as it directly impacts heat production within the system. Fig. 9a, b, c, and 9d illustrate the measurement positions and results of surface roughness for a single-bead multilayer wall. Due to significant size variations on the side of the single-bead multilayer wall caused by the step effect, analyzing surface roughness on this surface is not critical. Consequently, measurements were conducted solely on the top surface, revealing that the surface roughness Ra could reach as low as 0.142 μm.It is critical to clarify that the minimum Ra of 0.142 μm reported here is measured strictly on the top surface. This exceptional smoothness is not a natural result of wire melting but is achieved through the in-situ hot-burnishing (ironing) effect. The copper roller applies continuous mechanical pressure to the semi-molten metal during deposition, flattening the surface irregularities before final solidification, effectively acting as an integrated finishing process. The side surfaces, however, naturally retain the step-effect typical of layer-by-layer manufacturing. Fig. 9e and f elucidate the influences of different process parameters on the surface roughness Ra and Rz of a single-bead multilayer wall, as well as the section hardness. Both Ra and Rz initially decrease and then increase with increasing process parameters across the three groups. Conversely, the section hardness exhibits a non-monotonic trend. Initially, the hardness increases as the process parameters optimize the layer density. However, with excessive heat input, the hardness demonstrates a declining trend. This reduction in hardness (softening) under high heat input is attributed to the grain coarsening effect, governed by the Hall-Petch relationship. Prolonged residence at elevated temperatures and slower cooling rates in the vacuum environment promote grain growth, which reduces the density of grain boundaries and consequently lowers the mechanical resistance. Although detailed EBSD analysis is beyond the scope of this work, the observed hardness trends are consistent with these thermally-driven microstructural changes. Future microstructural investigations are required to definitively correlate these hardness variations with grain size evolution via the Hall-Petch relationship.Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 9(i), the three-dimensional perspective surface scan of the single-bead multilayer wall exhibits a continuous and flat surface morphology without obvious macroscopic surface defects such as un-melted gaps or severe distortion. This macroscopic integrity further substantiates the technological feasibility of the JHAM process.

Model validation

A key validation of the multiphysics model is the comparison of the electrical response. Fig. 6h plots the simulated and experimentally measured voltage across the roller and substrate during a deposition run at I = 120 A, V = 200 mm/min. The simulated voltage remains stable at ~600 mV, reflecting the steady-state contact resistance in the model. The experimental voltage fluctuates around this value (e.g., 550-650 mV), which is expected due to minor variations in real-time contact pressure and surface conditions.The fidelity of the multiphysics model is corroborated by both electrical and geometric responses. While the average voltage error remains below 10%, a comparative analysis of the simulated melt pool dimensions and the experimental bead cross-sections (as shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5) reveals a strong correlation in geometric trends. The model successfully predicts the expansion of the fusion zone with increasing current and its contraction with increasing speed, thereby validating its predictive capability for the JHAM process. Beyond the electrical response, the model’s fidelity is further corroborated by geometric trends. As demonstrated in both the simulated melt pool dimensions and the experimental cross-sections (Fig. 4 and Fig. 5), the predicted trends—where increasing current widens the fusion zone while increasing speed narrows it—show strong qualitative and quantitative agreement. This confirms that the model reliably captures the thermal-structural coupling during the deposition process.

Conclusion

This work presents a comprehensive investigation into Joule-Heating Additive Manufacturing (JHAM) through integrated multiphysics simulation and experimental validation. The main conclusions are:

  • The numerical model successfully predicts the formation of a distinctive "tusk-shaped" temperature field in multi-layer deposition, arising from the dynamic balance between localized Joule heating, heat conduction to the roller, and accumulation in the track.

  • Process parameters (current, speed, contact width) critically control peak temperature, melt pool size, and consequently, the geometric accuracy of the built wall. Optimal macroscopic morphology for 304 stainless steel was achieved at 120 A, 200 mm/min, and 1.0 mm width.

  • Geometric analysis reveals that increasing current promotes width and inter-layer fusion (overlap) but reduces height; increasing speed reduces width and overlap but can increase height; increasing contact width increases height but reduces width and overlap.

  • Surface roughness (Ra, Rz) and cross-sectional micro-hardness show non-monotonic relationships with process parameters, indicating the existence of optimal windows for surface finish and mechanical properties.The close match between simulated and experimental voltage signals validates the fidelity of the coupled electrical-thermal model.

  • The JHAM process demonstrates compelling attributes-low power (<1 kW), simplicity, and gravity independence for in-space manufacturing applications. Future work will focus on multi-material capabilities, micro-structure control, and the fabrication of more complex 3D geometries.

top.title: Differential effects of plyometric training loads on jump and sprint performance reveal optimal strategies
top.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 07 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-41245-4 Differential effects of plyometric training loads on jump and sprint performance reveal optimal strategies
top.content:
Abstract

Plyometric training is an effective method for enhancing lower-body muscle power, and modulating external loads may further optimize its training effects. Currently, there is a lack of systematic comparisons between externally loaded and unloaded plyometric training with respect to performance outcomes, such as vertical jump, horizontal jump, and sprint performance. Moreover, the differential effects of various load intensities remain unclear. Therefore, this study employs a meta-analysis to systematically evaluate the effects of plyometric training under different loads on jump and sprint performance. Following PRISMA guidelines, randomized controlled trials published between January 2010 and April 2025 were systematically searched in PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, EBSCO, and CNKI databases. Inclusion criteria were: healthy participants, an experimental group performing externally loaded plyometric training, a control group performing unloaded plyometric training, and outcome measures including jump and/or sprint performance. Fourteen studies were ultimately included. Externally loaded training was categorized into four groups based on load intensity: assisted load (APT, < body weight), low load (LPT, ≤ 5% body weight), medium load (MPT, 5–15% body weight), and high load (HPT, > 15% body weight). A meta-analysis was conducted using RevMan 5.4 software to pool effect sizes, test heterogeneity, perform subgroup analyses, and generate forest plots. Data were synthesized using random-effects or fixed-effects models, with effect sizes expressed as standardized mean differences. The significance level was set at p < 0.05. Vertical Jump: Only MPT significantly improved performance vs. UPT (ES = 0.43, p < 0.0001). APT, LPT, and HPT showed no significant difference. Horizontal Jump: No significant differences found among any load intensities vs. UPT (ES = 0.09, p = 0.40). Sprint: LPT (ES=-0.92, p = 0.0005) and MPT (ES=-0.23, p = 0.04) significantly improved performance vs. UPT. HPT showed no significant effect. Medium-load (5–15% body weight) plyometric training is most effective for improving vertical jump performance. Both low-load and medium-load training are more effective than unloaded training in enhancing sprint performance. Assisted-load training yields effects similar to those of unloaded training on jump performance. No significant differences were observed among various load intensities in improving horizontal jump performance. It is recommended to select appropriate load intensities based on specific performance goals in practice and to avoid excessive loading (i.e., 15% of body weight) to maintain movement quality and training adaptability.

Introduction

Muscle power refers to the ability to generate maximal muscular force in an extremely short period of time. It is a critical biological motor quality determining whether an athlete can gain an advantage in competition1. Among various competitive sports, superior sprinting speed and jumping height are the most direct manifestations of muscle power. Together, they form the physiological foundation for athletes to execute key techniques, realize tactical intentions, and ultimately determine competition outcomes2,3.

Different types of sports impose specialized and differentiated demands on sprinting and jumping abilities. The on-field performance in team sports (e.g., basketball, soccer, rugby) is highly dependent on athletes’ frequent execution of short-distance sprints, rapid changes of direction, and various forms of jumps during offense and defense. These abilities are fundamental for implementing tactics and creating scoring opportunities4. Individual sports (e.g., sprinting, long jump, tennis) more directly treat sprint speed and jump height as core performance indicators. Sprinting and long jump pursue ultimate speed and flight distance, while tennis and badminton rely on rapid sprints to reach the ball combined with jumps to execute overhead smashes5. Indoor sports (e.g., volleyball, indoor track and field, badminton), constrained by fixed courts and specific surface conditions, place more refined demands on the efficiency of sprint initiation, the vertical height of jumps, and the stability of consecutive jumps. Examples include consecutive blocking and spiking in volleyball, and the explosive start in indoor sprinting, both of which reflect the optimization of sprint and jump performance within limited space and under specific damping conditions6,7. These demand differences essentially correspond to distinct force-time characteristics within the muscle stretch-shortening cycle (SSC)8: Jumps aiming for maximum height (e.g., volleyball block, basketball rebound) often involve a “slow SSC,” allowing for longer ground contact time to accumulate impulse9; whereas actions pursuing speed and rapid force development (e.g., soccer change of direction, sprint start) rely on a “fast SSC,” emphasizing force development within an extremely short time frame10. This distinction provides clear guidance for the targeted development of the sprinting and jumping capacities required by different sports.

Plyometric training, by precisely simulating and intensifying the SSC, serves as a core training modality for enhancing athletes’ muscle power performance11,12. Its effectiveness stems not only from its physiological mechanism—namely, utilizing elastic energy and enhancing neural drive through rapid eccentric-concentric transitions13—but also from its high degree of adjustability14,15,16,17. Recent systematic reviews indicate that by carefully manipulating training variables, specific adaptations can be induced18,19. The study by Moran et al. (2021)20 on vertical and horizontal plyometric training showed that horizontal plyometric training (HPT) is at least as effective as vertical plyometric training (VPT) in improving vertical performance but superior in enhancing horizontal performance. The research by Chen et al. (2023)21 on plyometric training for adolescents suggested that when the training goal is to improve the Countermovement Jump (CMJ), the recommended total ground contact frequency (TGCF) should be kept below 900 contacts; when aiming to enhance the Squat Jump (SJ), the TGCF should exceed 1400 contacts. The total intervention duration is recommended to be between 400 and 600 min, with 500–600 min being optimal for CMJ and 400–500 min for SJ. The study by Zhang et al. (2025)22 on unilateral and bilateral plyometric training demonstrated that both unilateral (UNI) and bilateral (BI) plyometric training show specific advantages in improving single-leg jump, sprint, and change-of-direction performance, while BI training is more effective in optimizing bilateral jump performance. In summary, extensive systematic reviews have confirmed that by regulating movement patterns, ground contact times, and intervention duration, the effects of plyometric training can be precisely tailored to meet the biomechanical demands of different sports. However, manipulating the training stimulus by adjusting external load has been a recent research focus for optimizing training outcomes. This leads to a key question: Based on traditional bodyweight training, can adding (loaded) or reducing (assisted) external load further optimize, or even more precisely transfer to, specific sports performances?

Regarding the effects of different loading modalities, existing research findings are inconsistent. Assisted plyometric training is considered potentially more beneficial for developing neuromuscular adaptations suited to high-velocity movement patterns23; whereas loaded plyometric training aims to enhance force output by increasing resistance, potentially being more advantageous for actions requiring overcoming greater external resistance or pursuing maximal force24. However, results from comparative studies on improving jump performance and other metrics are often contradictory25,26. These discrepancies may stem from methodological differences in research, including limited sample sizes, inconsistent definitions of load intensity, and, most importantly, a lack of separate investigation into different performance indicators (e.g., vertical jump, horizontal jump, sprint). Currently, there is a lack of a systematic meta-analysis specifically designed to integrate and compare the differential effects of “externally loaded plyometric training” versus “plyometric training without external load” on these key performance metrics. Furthermore, the potentially specific dose-response relationships between different load intensity levels (e.g., assisted, low, moderate, high load) and these metrics remain unclear.

Therefore, to address the aforementioned gaps in the literature and provide more targeted scientific evidence for training practice, this study aims to systematically evaluate the effects of loaded plyometric training compared to unloaded plyometric training on vertical jump, horizontal jump, and sprint performance in healthy individuals through a meta-analysis. The specific objectives are: (1) To comprehensively compare the overall effect differences between the two training modalities on the aforementioned three categories of performance metrics; (2) Through subgroup analysis, to investigate whether different load intensity levels produce differential effects on vertical jump, horizontal jump, and sprint performance, with the aim of revealing their specific dose-response relationships. The results of this study are expected to provide key data support and decision-making basis for coaches and strength and conditioning specialists to scientifically and individually select and design load prescriptions for plyometric training according to the specific demands of athletes’ sports.

Methods

This meta-analysis strictly adhered to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines27. The study protocol was prospectively registered on the PROSPERO platform (Registration ID: CRD420251048056).

Search strategy

Two researchers independently performed a systematic literature search across five electronic databases, covering the period from January 1, 2010, to April 30, 2025. The search was conducted following a predefined strategy (see Table 1 for detailed search terms and syntax). Any discrepancies in search execution or results were resolved through discussion.

Inclusion and exclusion criteria

Inclusion criteria followed the PICOS framework:

  • 1.

    Participants: Healthy individuals, irrespective of sex or athletic level.

  • 2.

    Intervention: Experimental group receiving externally loaded plyometric training (EPT);

  • 3.

    Comparator: Control group performing unloaded plyometric training (UPT);

  • 4.

    Outcomes: Jump and/or sprint performance metrics;

  • 5.

    Study design: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs).

Exclusion criteria comprised:

  • 1.

    Non-RCTs, self-controlled trials, or crossover trials;

  • 2.

    Incomplete or inaccessible data;

  • 3.

    Conference abstracts, reviews, or meta-analyzes;

  • 4.

    Unavailable full-text articles;

  • 5.

    Studies involving non-healthy populations.

Literature screening and data extraction

Literature screening was conducted using EndNote X9, with data extraction performed in Excel. Two researchers independently screened studies and extracted data, followed by cross-verification. Discrepancies were resolved through consultation with a third researcher.

Extracted data included: (1) First author and publication year; (2) Participant characteristics (sample size per group, sex, age, height, body mass, sport, and training level); (3) Baseline and post-intervention data for outcome measures; (4) Training characteristics (modality, duration, frequency, session volume, sets, repetitions, ground contacts). All extracted data were verified by a second independent reviewer (F.M. for the initial screening phase.

Literature quality evaluation

Methodological quality was evaluated using the Physiotherapy Evidence Database (PEDro) scale. This 11-item tool rates study validity on a scale from 0 (high risk) to 10 (low risk), with scores ≥ 6 indicating low risk of bias and high quality. Two independent researchers conducted assessments, with disagreements arbitrated by a third researcher.

Publication bias

Publication bias was assessed using Egger’s test in Stata SE 12.0, with p < 0.05 indicating significant bias. For outcomes with a sufficient number of studies (n ≥ 10), funnel plots were visually inspected for asymmetry to complement the statistical test.

Statistical analysis

Data synthesis was performed using RevMan 5.4. An analysis is required for at least two comparable studies per outcome. All measurements were converted to SI units and standardized using unified formulas.

For continuous outcomes, standardized mean differences (SMD; Hedges’ g) were calculated as effect sizes28. Effect magnitudes were interpreted according to Cohen’s conventions: 0.2 = small, 0.5 = medium, 0.8 = large29.

Heterogeneity among studies was assessed using the I² statistic. An I² value of < 25% was considered low, 25–50% moderate, and > 50% substantial heterogeneity30. A random-effects model was used for all primary meta-analyses to account for anticipated clinical and methodological heterogeneity. A fixed-effect model was applied only in sensitivity analyses when heterogeneity was negligible (I² = 0%). The overall effect was considered statistically significant if the 95% confidence interval (CI) did not cross zero. Forest plots were generated to visualize individual study effects and pooled estimates.

Fixed-effect models (FE) were applied when I²<25%; random-effects models (RE) were used when I² ≥ 25%31. The random-effects model was employed as the primary analysis model to account for potential clinical and methodological heterogeneity across studies, with the fixed-effect model used in sensitivity analyses. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05.

Calculation formula:

The value of the coefficient R is 0.5.

The intervention groups were categorized into four subgroups based on the external load intensity relative to body mass: Assisted Plyometric Training (APT: load < body mass), Low Load Plyometric Training (LPT: load ≤ 5% body mass), Medium Load Plyometric Training (MPT: load > 5% and ≤ 15% body mass), and High Load Plyometric Training (HPT: load > 15% body mass). Separate meta-analyses were conducted for vertical jump, horizontal jump, and sprint performance outcomes, comparing each loaded subgroup to the unloaded plyometric training (UPT) control group from the same study.

Sensitivity analyses were performed by sequentially removing individual studies to assess the robustness of the pooled estimates, particularly for outcomes showing moderate or high heterogeneity (I² ≥ 50%).

Results

Literature screening

The initial database search identified 1,352 records. After deduplication, the search yielded the following across individual databases: PubMed (n = 247), Web of Science (n = 395), Scopus (n = 560), EBSCO (n = 99), and CNKI (n = 51). Ultimately, 14 studies met the inclusion criteria and were included in the meta-analysis (Fig. 1).

Study characteristics

The meta-analysis included 14 articles encompassing 37 distinct study arms. The pooled sample comprised 399 participants aged 13–23 years, predominantly athletes from soccer, basketball, tennis, volleyball, taekwondo, martial arts, and badminton. The intervention duration ranged from 5 to 12 weeks, with a training frequency of 2–3 sessions per week. Session duration varied between 20 and 90 min. Training and performance caliber (Tier 1: recreational, Tier 3: highly trained/national level, Tier 4: elite/international level) as described by McKay et al.32 (Table 2).

Risk of bias assessment

Based on the PEDro scale assessment, 10 studies scored ≥ 6 points, indicating low risk of bias and high methodological quality (Table 3).

Meta-analysis results

Vertical jump performance

A total of 43 datasets from 14 studies were analyzed (Fig. 2). Meta-analysis revealed that externally loaded plyometric training (EPT) significantly improved vertical jump performance compared to unloaded plyometric training (UPT) (ES = 0.21, 95% CI 0.08–0.34; Z = 3.12, p = 0.002), with negligible heterogeneity (I² = 0%, p = 0.65).

The subgroup analysis stratifying different load intensities of EPT showed that: Assisted Plyometric Training (APT): No significant difference vs. UPT (ES = 0.00, 95% CI − 0.420.41; Z = 0.01, p = 0.99; I² = 0%, p = 0.83); Low Plyometric Training (LPT): No significant difference vs. UPT (ES = 0.03, 95% CI − 0.23–0.29; Z = 0.20, p = 0.84; I² = 0%, p = 0.71); Medium Plyometric Training (MPT): Significantly greater improvement vs. UPT (ES = 0.43, 95% CI 0.24–0.62; Z = 4.37, p < 0.0001; I² = 0%, p = 0.57); High Plyometric Training (HPT): No significant difference vs. UPT (ES = 0.02, 95% CI − 0.30–0.33; Z = 0.10, p = 0.92; I² = 0%, p = 0.85).

Horizontal jump performance

Fourteen datasets from 7 studies were analyzed (Fig. 3). Meta-analysis showed no significant difference between EPT and UPT (ES = 0.09, 95% CI − 0.13 to 0.31; Z = 0.83, p = 0.40; I² = 0%, p = 0.88).

The subgroup analysis stratifying different load intensities of EPT showed that: LPT: No significant difference vs. UPT (ES = 0.17, 95% CI − 0.32 to 0.67; Z = 0.68, p = 0.50; I² = 0%, p = 0.50); MPT: No significant difference vs. UPT (ES = 0.08, 95% CI − 0.19 to 0.35; Z = 0.59, p = 0.56; I² = 0%, p = 0.85); HPT: No significant difference vs. UPT (ES = 0.03, 95% CI − 0.85 to 0.92; Z = 0.07, p = 0.94; I² = 46%, p = 0.17).

Sprint performance

Twenty-three datasets from 6 studies were analyzed (Fig. 4). Meta-analysis indicated that EPT significantly improved sprint performance compared to UPT (ES = − 0.41, 95% CI − 0.64 to − 0.17; Z = 3.35, p = 0.0008), with moderate heterogeneity (I² = 46%, p = 0.009).

The subgroup analysis stratifying different load intensities of EPT showed that: LPT: Significantly greater improvement vs. UPT (ES=− 0.92, 95% CI − 1.44 to − 0.40; Z = 3.47, p = 0.0005; I² = 65%, p = 0.006); MPT: Significantly greater improvement vs. UPT (ES = − 0.23, 95% CI − 0.46 to − 0.01; Z = 2.06, p = 0.04; I² = 0%, p = 0.98); HPT: No significant difference vs. UPT (ES = 0.37, 95% CI − 0.30 to 0.17; Z = 1.09, p = 0.28; I² = 0%, p = 0.67).

Sensitivity analysis

The observation of Fig. 3 revealed that, in horizontal jump performance, Li (2020) SLJ 30% and Tao (2020) DLB 20% were the primary sources of heterogeneity. However, because only two studies were included, the underlying reasons for this finding remain uncertain.

Publication bias

Egger’s test indicated no significant publication bias for vertical jump (p = 0.425), horizontal jump (p = 0.057), or sprint performance (p = 0.432) (Table 4).

Discussion

This study, through a systematic review and meta-analysis, systematically explored the intrinsic mechanisms of plyometric training with different loads and their differential effects on athletic performance. The results indicate that a moderate external load (specifically, a medium load of 5–15% body weight) can effectively enhance vertical jump performance by optimizing the muscle stretch-shortening cycle (SSC). The mechanisms primarily involve improving the storage of potential energy in elastic tissues like tendons, enhancing the efficiency of neural drive, and increasing tendon stiffness. However, there is a clear upper limit to the load (> 15% body weight). Overloading can disrupt the structure and function of the SSC, manifested as prolonged ground contact time and potentially triggered neural inhibition, thereby diminishing training benefits. The study further elucidates the root cause of the differential training effects: vertical jump and sprint performance rely on different types of SSC (slow vs. fast) and distinct neuromuscular demands (maximal strength vs. rate of force development), which fundamentally dictate their respective adaptability to different training loads. Concurrently, horizontal jump performance was not significantly influenced by additional vertical load, suggesting that its improvement depends on more complex multi-planar coordination and force transfer mechanisms.

Mechanisms of loaded plyometrics

Plyometric training enhances movement efficiency by effectively utilizing the muscle stretch-shortening cycle (SSC)13. The SSC is a continuous biomechanical and neurophysiological process comprising three main phases: the eccentric phase where agonist muscles are rapidly lengthened, the concentric phase where they contract, and a brief transition phase between them45. At the mechanical level, elastic tissues like tendons store elastic potential energy when rapidly stretched during the eccentric phase46,47,48; this stored energy is subsequently released rapidly during the ensuing concentric contraction, thereby augmenting total force output49. In loaded plyometric training, the increased load may enhance the degree and speed of stretch on the muscle-tendon complex during the eccentric phase, potentially storing more elastic energy.

From a neurophysiological perspective, the SSC utilizes the stretch reflex to potentiate muscle concentric force50,51. When a muscle is rapidly and forcibly stretched, muscle spindles are activated, leading to increased motor unit recruitment and/or firing frequency, which enhances concentric force52,53. As the training load increases, the nervous system adapts by improving conduction velocity, shortening reaction time, and preferentially recruiting high-threshold fast-twitch motor units, thereby increasing force output efficiency54. Ramírez-Campillo et al.55(2020) found that after 8 weeks of moderate-load plyometric training, athletes’ integrated electromyographic (iEMG) activity of the vastus lateralis increased by 18.7%, which was positively correlated with the improvement in jump height (12.5%), providing direct evidence of neural adaptation. Furthermore, long-term moderate-load training can induce structural adaptations in tendons, such as increased collagen fiber density and improved elastic modulus, leading to more efficient storage and release of elastic energy56. Malisoux et al.57 demonstrated that after 8 weeks of plyometric training, Achilles tendon stiffness increased by 14%, directly correlating with improvements in vertical jump height.

Therefore, within a moderate range, loaded plyometric training can produce superior effects compared to bodyweight training through the aforementioned mechanical, neural, and structural adaptive mechanisms. However, this optimization effect has a clear load threshold. When the external load exceeds approximately 15% of body weight, its positive effects diminish or even become negative. Acute experimental evidence supports this view: a study comparing the immediate neuromuscular responses to different external loads (0%, 10%, 15%, 20% body weight) during countermovement jumps found that a 10% body weight load was optimal for the rate of force development, while 15% and 20% body weight loads, although potentially enhancing neuromuscular efficiency at the ankle joint, may be approaching or exceeding the point of diminishing returns58.

The negative mechanisms of excessive load primarily include: First, excessive external load may cause unfavorable joint “collapse” or reduced stability during movement, disrupting the pre-stretch posture required for the SSC and significantly prolonging the ground contact time (GCT) during the transition phase, causing the elastic energy stored in the eccentric phase to dissipate before the concentric contraction begins59. Second, excessive load strongly stimulates the Golgi Tendon Organs (GTO) at the musculotendinous junction, triggering an autogenic inhibition reflex that inhibits the agonist while facilitating the antagonist. Although this is a protective mechanism, it directly impairs performance60,61. Finally, from a neuromuscular control perspective, excessive external load forces the central nervous system to adopt a stiffer, lower-degree-of-freedom control strategy to maintain stability, which limits the diversity and flexibility of multi-muscle synergy, thereby undermining the efficient and coordinated force production pattern upon which the SSC mechanism relies15.

Similar effects of plyometric training with different loads on horizontal jump performance

The improvement in horizontal jump performance showed no significant difference between loaded and unloaded plyometric training. This can be primarily attributed to the following two reasons:

First, differences in movement pattern specificity. Vertical jumps are predominantly centered on movements in the sagittal plane, whereas horizontal jumps require complex multi-planar coordination involving the sagittal, frontal, and even transverse planes62. Current common methods of loaded plyometric training often employ vertical loading (e.g., weight vests), lacking equipment that provides resistance in the horizontal direction. From a mechanical objective perspective, vertical jumps focus on rapidly generating vertical ground reaction force (GRF), whereas horizontal jumps require effectively redirecting the generated GRF vector forward, a process highly dependent on the eccentric-concentric coupling efficiency of muscles like the hip extensors in the horizontal direction63. Additional vertical load is not sufficiently specific for training this horizontal force generation and transfer mechanism64.

Second, it stems from the fundamentally different demand for neuromuscular coordination complexity in horizontal jumps. Horizontal jumps (e.g., standing long jump, triple jump) require a high degree of synergy across the whole-body kinetic chain within multiple planes such as the sagittal and frontal planes, making their neuromuscular control pattern more complex than that of vertical jumps. This is primarily manifested in: (1) Horizontal jumps require the generation of propulsive force at a lower take-off angle (typically < 45°)65, which strongly depends on the stabilizing role of core muscles (e.g., transversus abdominis, erector spinae) to achieve effective momentum transfer from the lower limbs to the upper body66. The significant promoting effect of core training on horizontal jump performance (effect size ES = 0.84) also corroborates this point67. (2) The take-off in horizontal jumps relies on a hip-dominant coordination pattern (the hip joint contributes approximately 45% of the vertical force, higher than the knee joint’s 30%)68 and demands precise inter-joint timing coordination. If core stability is insufficient, the force from hip extension may translate into spinal flexion rather than horizontal propulsion69. (3) Traditional vertically oriented loaded plyometric training may lead to an increased take-off angle (> 50°), which does not match the optimal angle for horizontal jumps (< 45°). Research indicates that for every 5° the take-off angle exceeds the optimal value, jump distance may decrease by 8–12%70.

Differential effects of loaded plyometrics on jump and sprint performance

In contrast to its effects on jump performance, low-load plyometric training (≤ 5% body weight) demonstrates a more pronounced effect on improving sprint performance. This difference in training efficacy stems from fundamental distinctions in the biomechanical mechanisms and neuromuscular adaptation requirements between jumping and sprinting. Specifically, this distinction manifests at two progressive levels: first, Fundamental Biomechanical Difference: Force Production Time Window and SSC Type. Jumping and sprinting have drastically different temporal demands for force production, which directly determines their respective SSC types. Based on the critical parameter of ground contact time, plyometric activities are classified as either slow SSC (GCT > 250 ms) or fast SSC (GCT < 250 ms), two modes that rely on distinct biomechanical mechanisms8,71. The vertical jump is a typical slow SSC activity. Its longer ground contact time (typically exceeding 250 ms) provides muscles with sufficient time to form more cross-bridge connections, thereby developing near-maximal force to ultimately maximize jump height72,73. Consequently, moderate-load training (5–15% body weight), which effectively optimizes maximal force production, is most suitable for such performance. In contrast, sprinting epitomizes the fast SSC, characterized by extremely brief ground contact times (~ 0.17 to 0.20s during acceleration, potentially under 0.11s at maximal speed)74,75,76. This forces the neuromuscular system to generate an extremely high rate of force development within a very limited time window77. A biological characteristic of elite sprinters is achieving shorter ground contact times for greater movement efficiency while maintaining high force output78. This inherently constrained, extremely brief time available for force development constitutes the primary biomechanical rationale for selecting low-load training. Second, Divergent Neuromuscular Adaptation Emphasis Resulting from Biomechanical Differences. The aforementioned biomechanical differences further lead to a divergence in the adaptive demands placed on the neuromuscular system for jumping versus sprinting. This divergence is clearly reflected in their respective positions on the force-velocity curve: jump performance lies closer to the maximal force end, whereas sprint performance lies closer to the velocity end. Consequently, their optimal training loads serve different physiological objectives: moderate loads primarily serve to enhance maximal strength, while low loads are more conducive to developing speed and rapid force production capacity79. At the neuromuscular level, this means the two performances rely on separable neuromuscular properties. Jump performance is highly dependent on maximal strength80, whereas sprint performance is more determined by the rate of force development (RFD)81. These two properties require distinct training stimuli for targeted development82. Low-load plyometric training, through high-velocity movement execution, can more effectively optimize motor unit firing frequency, synchronization, and muscle pre-activation levels—neural adaptations that form the basis for improving RFD and sprint performance83. Conversely, moderate-to-high load training is more conducive to recruiting a greater number of high-threshold motor units and promoting increases in muscle cross-sectional area, thereby more effectively enhancing maximal strength, which is more critical for jump performance84.

Practical implications and future directions

This meta-analysis provides evidence-based loading guidelines for training practice. To enhance vertical jump performance, priority should be given to moderate loads (5–15% body weight) to optimize force generation under slow SSC conditions. To develop sprint capability, emphasis should be placed on low-load or bodyweight training (≤ 5% body weight) to improve the rate of force development and adapt to its biomechanical time constraints. Regarding horizontal jumps, given the finding that additional vertical load does not significantly improve it, it is recommended to adjust the training focus in practice, potentially incorporating multi-planar, multi-directional sport-specific technical drills or resistance exercises (e.g., sled pulls) to more directly develop horizontal propulsive force. In all training, excessive loading (> 15% body weight) must be strictly avoided, with movement quality prioritized and monitored. Future applications could explore more specialized loading modalities and verify their long-term effects.

Conclusion

The meta-analysis indicates that:

  • 1.

    Medium-load plyometric training (MPT; 5–15% body weight) is more effective than unloaded training for improving vertical jump performance.

  • 2.

    Both low-load (LPT; ≤5% body weight) and medium-load (MPT) plyometric training are superior to unloaded training for enhancing sprint performance.

  • 3.

    Assisted plyometric training (APT) shows similar effects to unloaded training on jump performance.

  • 4.

    No significant differences exist among load intensities for horizontal jump performance.

In practice, practitioners should select external loads based on specific performance goals—preferring MPT for vertical jump and LPT/MPT for sprint performance—while avoiding loads exceeding 15% body weight to maintain movement quality.

top.title: Scientists just created the most lifelike cell ever made in a lab — here's what it could accomplish
top.summary: SpudCell is a new cell-like platform that can feed, grow and divide like a normal cell — but it's not yet a perfect re-creation of the real thing.
top.content:
Scientists say they have built a "synthetic cell" that can eat, grow and divide in a way that's remarkably similar to living cells.

The research, released to the preprint database bioRxiv July 2, has not been peer-reviewed yet. It introduces SpudCell, a new type of artificial cell, and marks a striking step toward creating living cells from scratch. But for study co-author Kate Adamala, a synthetic biologist at the University of Minnesota, that's far from the most interesting part of the work.

"I do not believe [SpudCell] is alive," Adamala told Live Science. Instead, she describes the system as a framework that could generate "all the chemicals we need for our civilization with biology." The thought is that SpudCell could serve as a tiny factory, pumping out medicines, fertilizers, plastics or any number of other compounds.

The work's announcement has sparked some controversy, with some scientists seeing it as a ploy to gain media attention for the simultaneous launch of the author's nonprofit Biotic, which aims to raise money to further develop the SpudCell platform. Adamala does not take umbrage to that criticism, as she is keen to bring more attention and funding to her field. If a synthetic cell can be perfected, she thinks, it could help humanity generate chemicals without relying as heavily on petroleum products.

"I feel this incredible stressful urgency that if we don't get to work on it now, then we're going to run out of time," she said, referring to the climate crisis. "We need to highlight that bioengineering can offer a solution. That's why I'm doing it."

A synthetic cell and a big promise

According to the preprint, Adamala and her team produced a lifelike system that closely resembles a living cell. To do so, they combined 36 purified enzymes and a fatty membrane with a pared-back genome about 50 times smaller than that of an average bacterial cell. By mixing these human-made elements together, the scientists generated a cell that could feed, grow, and divide — so, in essence, they created a full cell cycle in a petri dish.

"We built a cell-like system that is fully chemically defined, so there are no unknown building blocks in it," Adamala said. "It's capable of doing things that people up until now used to think only natural living cells can do." They call the system "SpudCell" because it looks similar to a potato, The New York Times reported. The name is also a nod to the Sputnik satellite, per CNN.

Get the world’s most fascinating discoveries delivered straight to your inbox.

The concept of recreating the cell cycle in a dish is not entirely new. The J. Craig Venter Institute's 2016 "minimal cell" paper flirted with the concept by stripping as many genes from a bacterium as possible to leave only a minimal cell that could still replicate. However, the new study is the first time scientists have achieved feeding, growing and division using a "bottom-up" approach.

The work marks "a great advancement," said Mauro Rinaldi, a lecturer in biotechnology and biochemistry at the University of Hull in the U.K. who was not involved in the work. "It moves the needle because one of the key things about cells is division."

But there are important caveats. For one, the cells cannot yet create their own energy as our cells do with mitochondria. They also rely on externally provided fats, sugars and enzymes. The cells cannot make their own ribosomes, the machinery that turns genetic instructions into working parts of the cell. That means it relies on proteins being delivered from the outside.

Another difference is that SpudCell's genome is spread out over bits of DNA called plasmids, rather than being neatly packaged in chromosomes. It does not possess the skeleton that cells typically use to neatly split DNA during cell division, so consequently, the division of SpudCell's DNA to its daughter cells can be somewhat haphazard.

"The description of the results leaves me with substantial technical questions regarding the nature and the robustness of the findings," said Cees Dekker, a biophysicist at the Delft University of Technology in the Netherlands who was not involved in the work. "[Its] approach uses some ingenious engineering tricks as shortcuts to achieve complex functions such as growth, but a major challenge remains to create a truly autonomous cell that executes all these functions without external help," he said.

Dekker is among the scientists who would have preferred that the research made the news after being published in a peer-reviewed scientific journal. "If these findings are solid, that great media attention is definitely warranted; if peer review would, however, reveal weaknesses or issues, all the current media reporting is premature," he argued.

From cell to manufacturing platform

Adamala sees SpudCell as a blank slate for engineering. "We believe that if we make a cell from scratch, that's fully engineerable," she said.

Cells have long been used to produce chemicals for human use. Millions of people use synthetic insulin made in bacteria and yeast cells, for example. But cells resist making chemicals that could be harmful to them, and through evolution, they've developed systems to prevent them from doing so. A SpudCell-like system could circumvent those natural hurdles, Adamala and her colleagues think.

That kind of platform could also be useful for making newer generations of medicines, such as those based on mRNA or peptides. These drugs use molecular building blocks, like amino acids or nucleotides, that have been chemically tweaked to make the molecules more stable or harder for the body to break down. SpudCell could be engineered to produce such modified components directly, rather than scientists having to synthesize them in traditional chemistry labs. This could potentially shorten development timelines and lower costs, the team thinks.

Adamala and her colleagues also envision the cells being used as easily shippable laboratories. They could be dried out, shipped, stored without refrigeration, and then activated on-site to make chemicals, vaccines or proteins when and where they are needed.

But there's still a long road ahead. For now, SpudCell is only a proof of principle, and many hurdles must be addressed before it could become an industrial platform.

Related stories

"[SpudCell] is not an engineering platform that can give you any useful product, but it's step one," Adamala said. "It's probably at least a couple of decades from now when we can actually scale it up to the point where we can replace all the petrochemicals with biology, but I do believe it's doable."

Adamala hopes the nonprofit Biotic will help funnel donations from philanthropists directly to research. "Biotic is a funding agency that is going to globally fund this work," she said.

While the technology is promising, "it needs to go through peer review," Rinaldi said. "I expect a lot of the hype and some of the terms that they're using to go away after a couple of years have passed."

Marianne is a freelance science journalist specializing in health, space, and tech. She particularly likes writing about obesity, neurology, and infectious diseases, but also loves digging into the business of science and tech. Marianne was previously a news editor at The Lancet and Nature Medicine and the U.K. science reporter for Business Insider. Before becoming a writer, Marianne was a scientist studying how the body fights infections from malaria parasites and gut bacteria.

top.title: A two-step tilt compensation method for off-axis holographic displays
top.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 06 August 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-55232-2 A two-step tilt compensation method for off-axis holographic displays
top.content:
Abstract

Accurate diffraction between non-parallel planes is essential for holographic displays employing tilted spatial light modulators (SLMs). However, applying the standard angular spectrum method (ASM) directly to a tilted plane leads to geometric distortion and spectral aliasing. We present a two-step propagation method and its validation by experiments. The proposed method combines the standard ASM with a spatial-domain rotational transformation. The target image is first back-propagated to a parallel intermediate plane, then geometrically remapped onto the tilted SLM with phase correction. Implemented on an off-axis holographic display with \(30^\circ\) and \(45^\circ\) tilted SLM which leads to a large steering angle between the incident and reflected beam, the method is evaluated with multi-depth and multi-angle reconstructions. Results show improved image fidelity, stable depth performance, and robust compensation of off-axis holographic displays.

top.title: Frontiers | Correlation of UGT1A1 genotypes with newborn hyperbilirubinemia using newborn genetic screening
top.summary: BackgroundThis study evaluated whether uridine diphosphate glucuronosyltransferase 1A1 (UGT1A1) variants identified through newborn genetic screening are ass...
top.content:
Abstract

Background:

This study evaluated whether uridine diphosphate glucuronosyltransferase 1A1 (UGT1A1) variants identified through newborn genetic screening are associated with the development of clinically significant neonatal hyperbilirubinemia.

Methods:

Next-generation sequencing (NGS) was performed in 1,019 newborns, and pathogenic (P)/likely pathogenic (LP) UGT1A1 findings were confirmed by Sanger sequencing. This single-center cohort study was based on routine NBGS and bilirubin assessments, whereas genotype–phenotype correlations were analyzed retrospectively using routinely collected clinical data. Newborns with confirmed biallelic or multi-allelic UGT1A1 variants were grouped as homozygous (Hom), compound heterozygous (CH, confirmed in trans), or three-loci (3 loci, confirmed in trans), and were compared with a randomly selected control group without UGT1A1 variants. TcB levels were measured at prespecified timepoints (postnatal days 1, 4, and 42).

Results:

Seventy-five newborns had confirmed biallelic or multi-allelic UGT1A1 variants (Hom, n = 45; CH, n = 26; 3 loci, n = 4), and 40 newborns served as controls. The most frequent variants were c.211G>A and c.-41_-40dup; c.1091C>T and c.686C>A were less common. TcB values did not differ across groups on days 1 and 4, but were higher in all mutation groups than in controls on day 42 (P < 0.001), with the highest values observed in the 3-loci group. Clinically significant neonatal hyperbilirubinemia, defined as clinician-documented hyperbilirubinemia requiring phototherapy during the neonatal period, was rare (four cases in total; 3/75 in the mutation group and 1/40 in controls; P = 0.367), although the small number of events precludes firm between-group inference.

Conclusions:

In this single-center cohort, common UGT1A1 variants were associated with mildly elevated TcB levels at 6 weeks, suggesting slower postnatal bilirubin clearance, but showed little association with clinically significant neonatal hyperbilirubinemia. These findings support a variant-level reporting strategy for newborn genetic screening (NBGS). Routinely classifying common UGT1A1 variants (c.211G>A, c.-41_-40dup, c.1091C>T, and c.686C>A) as moderate-/high-risk and performing routine segregation analysis for multi-locus variants would likely increase genetic counseling burden and parental anxiety while providing minimal additional clinical benefit.

Introduction

Newborn genetic screening (NBGS) is a rapid, simple, and sensitive diagnostic approach used during the neonatal period. It plays a crucial role in detecting congenital and hereditary disorders that may cause physical or neurodevelopmental impairment in children. Early detection enables timely intervention before symptoms appear, thereby preventing irreversible damage and potentially saving lives (). In addition to traditional biochemical testing for suspected hereditary metabolic diseases, next-generation sequencing (NGS) can be used to screen for monogenic disorders. NGS shortens diagnostic time, predicts disease risk and carrier status, and supports precision medicine. Since 2022, China has promoted and published an expert consensus describing the principles for selecting diseases for NGS-based screening of monogenic disorders (–).

Studies of the uridine diphosphate glucuronosyltransferase 1A1 (UGT1A1) gene have yielded noteworthy insights. Variants are described according to Human Genome Variation Society (HGVS) nomenclature using the reference transcript UGT1A1 NM_000463.3. Hereditary nonhemolytic hyperbilirubinemia caused by UGT1A1 variants includes three clinically defined entities: Crigler–Najjar syndrome type I (CNS-I), Crigler–Najjar syndrome type II (CNS-II), and Gilbert syndrome (GS). Diagnosis is based on serum bilirubin levels and clinical manifestations. CNS-I is characterized by serum bilirubin levels of 20–45 mg/dL. Patients with little or no UGT1A1 activity may develop bilirubin encephalopathy and may die; survivors often require liver transplantation. CNS-II is characterized by serum bilirubin levels of 6–20 mg/dL with reduced UGT1A1 activity. Some patients with CNS-II develop severe hyperbilirubinemia with neurological sequelae, whereas others experience only mild symptoms. Phenobarbital and phototherapy can be used to treat CNS-II. GS is associated with serum bilirubin levels of 1–20 mg/dL and usually does not pose a major health threat, although some patients may experience fatigue, mild jaundice, or gastrointestinal symptoms (–). Most previous studies have been phenotype-driven and have analyzed gene variants only in visibly jaundiced patients. The present study evaluated UGT1A1 screening in NBGS by exploring the relationship between genotype and clinical manifestations. We also examined whether UGT1A1 screening should be included in NBGS.

Materials and methods

Ethical approval and consent to participate

This single-center cohort study was approved by the Ethical Committee of the Institute of Haidian District Maternal and Child Health Care Hospital (No. 2024-01). Written informed consent was obtained from the guardians for publication of relevant clinical information for academic purposes.

Newborns' general information

A total of 1,019 newborns who underwent screening for high-incidence monogenic diseases between July 2022 and November 2023 at Haidian District Maternal and Child Health Care Hospital were selected for analysis (see Table 1). The inclusion criteria were full-term singleton newborns with no structural or chromosomal abnormalities identified prenatally by ultrasound. NBGS was performed in newborns whose guardians provided informed consent to participate in the hospital's NBGS program. On the basis of NBGS results, newborns were categorized into four groups according to the type of UGT1A1 variants: the homozygous variant group (Hom), the compound heterozygous variant group (CH), the group carrying three alleles (3 loci), and the control group (without UGT1A1 variants). The control pool consisted of newborns without UGT1A1 variants who were born between July 2022 and February 2023, yielding a total of 546 newborns. Forty newborns were selected from these 546 using the even-ending random number table method. The present study is a retrospective analysis of prospectively collected routine clinical measurements, including transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB) at prespecified timepoints. Detailed data on several established non-genetic contributors to neonatal hyperbilirubinemia (e.g., feeding type, hemolysis, glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase [G6PD] status, and birth trauma) were not uniformly available and therefore were not adjusted for in the analyses.

Table 1

Group Number (n) Sex Gestational age (d) Neonate weight (g) Male Female Hom 45 21 24 275.8 ± 7.5 3,410.8 ± 347.28 CH 26 14 12 270.9 ± 6.8 3,185.8 ± 376.10 3 loci 4 1 3 266.2 ± 9.3 3,270.0 ± 530.85 Control 40 21 19 276.4 ± 13.9 3,288.0 ± 394.61 P-value 0.695 0.051 0.108

Patient demographics.

Hom, homozygous; CH, compound heterozygosity; 3 loci, newborns carrying three UGT1A1 allelic variants.

NBGS

NBGS was performed at 3 days of age. Genomic DNA was extracted from dried blood spots prepared from three drops of newborn heel blood using the QIAamp DNA Mini Kit (Qiagen, China). DNA libraries were prepared according to Illumina protocols, including end repair, adapter ligation, and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) enrichment. The amplified DNA was then captured using a whole-exome capture kit (MyGenostics GenCap Enrichment Technologies). Biotinylated capture probes were designed to tile all exons outside repetitive regions. Captured DNA was eluted and amplified for 13 cycles using the following program: 95 °C for 4 min (one cycle); 98 °C for 30 s, 65 °C for 30 s, and 72 °C for 30 s (13 cycles); and 72 °C for 5 min (one cycle). The PCR product was purified using SPRI beads (Beckman Coulter). The enrichment libraries were sequenced on a DNBSEQ-T7 platform with 150-bp paired-end reads. Variant interpretation followed the 2015 ACMG/AMP guidelines for sequence variant classification. Pathogenic (P) and likely pathogenic (LP) UGT1A1 variants were selected according to these criteria; the corresponding evidence codes and supporting data, including published functional evidence and in-trans parental confirmation when available, are provided.

Sanger sequencing

Sanger sequencing was used to confirm P/LP variants. Parental blood samples were analyzed to verify the allelic origin. Newborns with variants confirmed in trans were included in the mutation group as compound heterozygotes. Hom variants were included in the mutation group without additional phase confirmation.

Comparison of neonatal TcB values

TcB values on postnatal days 1, 4, and 42 were measured using the JH20-1C transcutaneous jaundice detector (Nanjing University of Science and Technology Technology Development Co., Ltd.; Registration No. Su Xie Zhu Zhun 20162221152) (Table 2).

Table 2

Group Day 1 (mg/dL) Day 4 (mg/dL) Day 42 (mg/dL) Hom (n = 45) 5.9 ± 3.0 12.0 ± 1.6 5.5 ± 2.3 (2.2-17.1) (7.8–15.7) (1.1–10.9) CH (n = 26) 5.0 ± 1.0 12.9 ± 1.7 6.1 ± 2.2 (3.0–7.5) (8.7–15.7) (2.8–9.7) 3 loci (n = 4) 5.7 ± 1.9 13.2 ± 0.6 8.6 ± 2.4 (3.2–7.5) (12.3–13.6) (5.8–11.3) Control (n = 40) 5.1 ± 1.3 12.7 ± 2.2 4.2 ± 2.3 (1.4–7.5) (4.6–16.8) (0.8–10.2)

Tcb values.

TcB, transcutaneous bilirubin. Values are presented as mean ± SD (range).

The scheduling of TcB measurements on postnatal days 1, 4, and 42 was designed on the basis of the physiological progression of neonatal jaundice and established clinical practice. Day 1 measurements provide an early baseline and help identify rapid bilirubin increases that may indicate hemolytic disease or other early-onset pathological conditions (). Day 4 corresponds to the critical window for predischarge or early post-discharge risk assessment, because serum bilirubin typically peaks between 72 and 96 h of age in term and late-preterm newborns (). Day 42 was selected because it corresponds to the routine postnatal follow-up visit in China (). Assessment at this timepoint is useful for documenting the resolution of visible jaundice and identifying prolonged hyperbilirubinemia, such as breast milk jaundice or delayed bilirubin clearance potentially influenced by UGT1A1 genetic variants, after the initial physiological phase has passed ().

Outcome definitions and post-positive clinical workflow

For outcome classification, routine clinical records were reviewed together with available total serum bilirubin results. Clinically significant neonatal hyperbilirubinemia was operationally defined as clinician-documented jaundice requiring phototherapy during the neonatal period. TcB was used as a screening measure and was not used alone to define severe hyperbilirubinemia. Physiologic jaundice was defined as jaundice appearing after the first 24 h of life, managed without phototherapy or exchange transfusion, showing spontaneous improvement on routine follow-up, and, when serum bilirubin was measured, remaining below the level considered to require treatment in routine care. Serum bilirubin testing was obtained only when clinically indicated by the treating neonatologist and was not performed uniformly in all newborns with elevated TcB values. Clinical decisions regarding serum bilirubin testing and initiation of phototherapy were made as part of routine neonatal care rather than according to a study-mandated standardized bilirubin threshold. Therefore, the outcome comparison in this study was based primarily on clinical management categories rather than on uniform serum bilirubin-based biochemical classification for the entire cohort.

After an NBGS-positive UGT1A1 finding, the routine workflow included disclosure of the result to guardians, confirmatory Sanger sequencing, parental testing for segregation analysis when multi-allelic findings were suspected, and routine clinical follow-up based on the neonatologist's assessment. This workflow increased counseling time and family communication workload, particularly for common variant combinations with uncertain immediate clinical impact.

Statistical methods

SPSS (version 26.0) was used for statistical analyses. Categorical variables were summarized as counts and percentages and compared using the chi-square test; Fisher's exact test was used when expected cell counts were small. Continuous variables were summarized as mean ± standard deviation (mean ± SD). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used for overall comparisons of TcB values across the four groups, and pairwise between-group comparisons were performed for prespecified contrasts (Table 3).

Table 3

Comparison of TcB values.

The P values in the “Four groups” row represent overall between-group comparisons (one-way ANOVA), whereas the remaining P values represent pairwise comparisons.

Bold values indicate statistically significant differences (P < 0.05).

Because the 3-loci group was very small (n = 4), ANOVA-based results involving this group should be interpreted as exploratory; this limitation is acknowledged. The significance level was set at α = 0.05, and P-values <0.05 were considered statistically significant. No formal a priori sample size calculation was performed because this was an exploratory single-center study based on all eligible NBGS participants during the study period and the available follow-up cohort.

Results

Comparison of general information of newborns in four groups

Our study included 1,019 newborns. In the final dataset used for grouping (Figure 1), 546 newborns had no detected UGT1A1 variants and 473 had at least one UGT1A1 variant. Of these, 121 had two or more P/LP allelic variants. Among these 121 newborns, 45 were Hom, 64 had two heterozygous variants (including 26 confirmed in trans), and 12 had three variants (including four confirmed in trans). Accordingly, the Hom group contained 45 cases, the CH group 26 cases, and the 3-loci group four cases (see Figure 1). The NBGS-positive rate was 75/1,019 (7.4%). Forty newborns without UGT1A1 variants were randomly selected as controls from the 546 newborns without detected UGT1A1 variants using the even-ending random number table method. The four groups did not differ significantly in sex, gestational age, or birth weight (P-values > 0.05; see Table 1).

Figure 1

LP UGT1A1 variants

Four LP variants classified according to the American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics (ACMG) guidelines () were identified in the mutation groups: c.211G>A, c.-41_-40dup, c.1091C>T, and c.686C>A. The allele frequencies were 9.7% for c.211G>A, 1.8% for c.-41_-40dup, 0.3% for c.1091C>T, and 0.5% for c.686C>A. The carrier rates (n = 352) were 59.9% for c.211G>A, 28.1% for c.-41_-40dup, 9.9% for c.1091C>T, and 0.6% for c.686C>A. The corresponding East Asian population frequencies in gnomAD were 0.153 for c.211G>A, 0.12210 for c.-41_-40dup, 0.01168 for c.1091C>T, and 0.01949 for c.686C>A (–) (see Table 4).

Day-42 TcB levels across four groups

TcB values on postnatal days 1, 4, and 42 were compared across the four groups. No statistically significant differences were observed on days 1 and 4 (P-values > 0.05; Figure 2 and Table 3). Although no significant between-group difference was observed on day 4, mean TcB values across groups ranged from 12.0 to 13.2 mg/dL (Table 2). These day-4 TcB values indicate that some infants were within a clinically relevant screening range; however, because confirmatory serum bilirubin testing was not performed uniformly in all infants with elevated TcB values, these TcB findings should not be interpreted as direct biochemical evidence of treatment-threshold or severe hyperbilirubinemia. By contrast, TcB values differed significantly across the four groups on day 42. In Table 3, the P-value in the “Four groups” row represents the overall between-group comparison, whereas the remaining P values represent pairwise comparisons. Compared with controls, all three mutation groups had higher day-42 TcB values (P-values <0.05). Pairwise comparisons among the mutation groups showed that the mean day-42 TcB values in the Hom group (5.5 ± 2.3 mg/dL) and CH group (6.1 ± 2.2 mg/dL) were lower than that in the 3-loci group (8.6 ± 2.5 mg/dL) (Table 2). Given the small sample size of the 3-loci group (n = 4), these findings should be interpreted cautiously.

Figure 2

Day-42 TcB levels by UGT1A1 genotype

Seven UGT1A1 genotypes identified by Sanger sequencing were analyzed to explore the association between genotype and day-42 TcB value. No statistically significant association between day-42 TcB value and genotype category was observed (P-value >0.05; Table 5). Likewise, no statistically significant difference in day-42 TcB value was observed between the Hom and CH groups (P-value >0.05).
